perturbation.original_text: The term “Person” includes the media and any corporation, partnership, group, individual or other entity.
perturbation.changed_text: The term “Person” includes any individual but excludes corporations, partnerships, groups, and other entities.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Person' aligns with legal norms, encompassing various entities. The modified definition excludes corporations and other entities, which contradicts standard legal interpretations and could limit liability or rights granted to non-individuals.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts 1 U.S. Code § 1, which states that the word 'person' includes corporations, companies, associations, firms, partnerships, societies, and joint stock companies, as well as individuals, unless the context indicates otherwise. The modified contract explicitly excludes these entities, creating a direct conflict with the statutory definition.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 1 U.S. Code § 1 - Words denoting number, gender, and so forth | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 1 U.S. Code § 1 - Words denoting number, gender, and so forth U.S. Code Notes prev | next In determining the meaning of any Act of Congress , unless the context indicates otherwise— words importing the singular include and apply to several persons , parties, or things; words importing the plural include the singular; words importing the masculine gender include the feminine as well; words used in the present tense include the future as well as the present; the words “ insane ” and “ insane person ” shall include every idiot, insane person , and person non compos mentis; the words “ person ” and “whoever” include corporations, companies, associations, firms, partnerships, societies, and joint stock companies, as well as individuals ; “ officer ” includes any person authorized by law to perform the duties of the office; “ signature ” or “ subscription ” includes a mark when the person making the same intended it as such; “ oath ” includes affirmation, and “ sworn ” includes affirmed; “ writing ” includes printing and typewriting and reproductions of visual symbols by photographing, multigraphing, mimeographing, manifolding, or otherwise. (July 30, 1947, ch. 388, 61 Stat. 633 ; June 25, 1948, ch. 645, § 6, 62 Stat. 859 ; Oct. 31, 1951, ch. 655, § 1, 65 Stat. 710 ; Pub. L. 112–231, § 2(a) , Dec. 28, 2012 , 126 Stat. 1619 .) Editorial Notes Amendments 2012— Pub. L. 112–231 , in fifth clause after opening clause, struck out “and ‘lunatic’ ” before “shall include every” and “lunatic,” before “insane person, ”. 1951—Act Oct. 31, 1951 , substituted, in fourth clause after opening clause, “used” for “use”. 1948—Act June 25, 1948 , included “tense”, “whoever”, “signature” , “subscription” , “writing” and a broader definition of “person” . Statutory Notes and Related Subs
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts 1 U.S. Code § 1. The original definition of 'Person' included corporations, partnerships, and other entities, aligning with the law. The changed definition explicitly excludes these entities. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that 'the words “person” and “whoever” include corporations, companies, associations, firms, partnerships, societies, and joint stock companies, as well as individuals'. This exclusion creates a direct conflict with the statutory definition, making the contract non-compliant and contradicting the legally recognized definition of 'person'. The exclusion is not a minor adjustment; it fundamentally alters who is considered a 'person' under the contract, conflicting with the statute.

perturbation.original_text: Each Recipient agrees that it and its Representatives will not to use any Evaluation Material of the Provider in violation of applicable securities laws.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Recipient agrees that it and its Representatives may use any Evaluation Material of the Provider, regardless of applicable securities laws.
perturbation.explanation: The original text prevents using evaluation material against security laws; this ensures compliance. By modifying it to 'regardless of' securities laws, it facilitates insider trading.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 15 U.S. Code § 78j - Manipulative and deceptive devices
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text directly contradicts 15 U.S. Code § 78j, which prohibits the use of manipulative and deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. This includes using non-public information obtained during the evaluation process for insider trading, which the original text sought to prevent.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 78j - Manipulative and deceptive devices | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 78j - Manipulative and deceptive devices U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next It shall be unlawful for any person , directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange— (a) (1) To effect a short sale , or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulations as the Commission may prescribe as necessary or appropriate in the public interest or for the protection of investors. (2) Paragraph (1) of this subsection shall not apply to security futures products . (b) To use or employ, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security registered on a national securities exchange or any security not so registered, or any securities-based swap agreement [1] any manipulative or deceptive device or contrivance in contravention of such rules and regulations as the Commission may prescribe as necessary or appropriate in the public interest or for the protection of investors. (c) (1) To effect, accept, or facilitate a transaction involving the loan or borrowing of securities in contravention of such rules and regulations as the Commission may prescribe as necessary or appropriate in the public interest or for the protection of investors. (2) Nothing in paragraph (1) may be construed to limit the authority of the appropriate Federal banking agency (as defined in section 1813(q) of title 12 ), the National Credit Union Administration , or any other Federal department or agency having a responsibility under Federal law to prescribe rules or regulations restricting transactions involving the loan or borrowing of securities in or
perturbation.justification: The modified text states that Recipients may use evaluation material regardless of applicable securities laws. This directly contradicts 15 U.S. Code § 78j, which prohibits manipulative and deceptive devices. The original text ensured compliance with these laws, while the changed text explicitly disregards them, facilitating potential illegal activities such as insider trading. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states "It shall be unlawful for any person , directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange— (b) To use or employ, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security registered on a national securities exchange or any security not so registered...any manipulative or deceptive device or contrivance in contravention of such rules and regulations as the Commission may prescribe as necessary or appropriate in the public interest or for the protection of investors." The changed text permits actions that would be unlawful under this statute, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is for the benefit of each Company and shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware applicable to agreements made and to be performed entirely within such state.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is for the benefit of each Company and shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of a jurisdiction chosen at the Provider's sole discretion, regardless of where the agreement is made or performed.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies Delaware law. The modified version introduces uncertainty by allowing the Provider to choose any jurisdiction, even if unrelated to the agreement, potentially circumventing established legal precedents and protections under Delaware law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware Code Title 6, § 2708. Choice of law.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts Delaware Code Title 6, § 2708, which generally respects parties' choice of law in contracts but implies a reasonable relationship to the transaction. Allowing the Provider unfettered discretion to choose any jurisdiction, regardless of its connection to the agreement, undermines the predictability and enforceability intended by Delaware law.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies Delaware law, while the modified version allows the Provider to choose any jurisdiction. This contradicts the intention of predictability and enforceability under Delaware law, as explained in the Law Explanation. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that this change introduces uncertainty and could circumvent legal precedents. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Delaware Code Title 6, Chapter 27, generally respects the parties' choice of law. However, allowing the Provider unfettered discretion undermines the reasonable relationship implied by Delaware law, as the chosen jurisdiction might have no connection to the agreement.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall terminate five years after the date of this Agreement (except with respect to trade secrets for which the term shall be perpetual).
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall terminate only upon the Potential Buyer's explicit written consent, irrespective of the passage of time or the nature of the information involved.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies a five-year term with perpetual protection for trade secrets. By making the termination solely dependent on the Potential Buyer's consent, the agreement could effectively become perpetual for all information, not just trade secrets, and unduly burden the Provider.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification could violate the principle against unreasonable restraints on trade, as reflected in Restatement (First) of Contracts § 178, particularly comment c. Although not a statute, this restatement section is a persuasive authority regarding the enforceability of agreements that unduly restrict a party's freedom. Making the termination solely dependent on the Potential Buyer’s consent could be viewed as an unreasonable restraint, especially if it covers information beyond trade secrets and continues indefinitely.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specified a definite term (five years) and perpetual protection for trade secrets. The changed text makes the termination solely dependent on the Potential Buyer's consent, which could lead to a perpetual restriction even for information that is not trade secrets, potentially violating principles against unreasonable restraints on trade as explained by the LLM. While the scraped snippets are not particularly helpful, the Law Explanation refers to Restatement (First) of Contracts § 178, comment c, as a persuasive authority regarding agreements that unduly restrict a party's freedom. This supports the assessment that the changed text introduces a potential legal contradiction because it could unreasonably restrain the Provider.
